news_summary: Today's technology news highlights major advances in quantum communication, innovative chemical processes, enhanced organic solar energy efficiency, and cutting-edge quantum computing research.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine de la communication quantique, des procédés chimiques innovants, une efficacité énergétique accrue des cellules solaires organiques et des recherches de pointe en informatique quantique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan importantes avances en el ámbito de la comunicación cuántica, procesos químicos innovadores, una mayor eficiencia de la energía solar orgánica y la investigación de vanguardia en computación cuántica.

articles.title: A photon was teleported across 270 meters in stunning quantum breakthrough
articles.summary: Scientists have pulled off a first: teleporting a photon’s state between two separate quantum dots. This was done over a 270-meter open-air link, proving quantum information can travel between independent devices. The achievement marks a key step toward building quantum networks for ultra-secure communication. It also sets the stage for more advanced systems like quantum relays.
articles.content:
An international team of researchers, including scientists from Paderborn University, has reached an important milestone on the path toward a quantum internet. For the first time, they successfully teleported the polarization state of a single photon from one quantum dot to another that was physically separated. In simple terms, this means the properties of one photon were transferred to another through quantum teleportation.

This achievement is a key step for future quantum communication networks. In the experiment, researchers used a 270m free-space optical link to connect the systems. The findings have been published in the journal Nature Communications.

A Decade of Collaboration Pays Off

At Paderborn University, doctoral and postdoctoral researchers spent about ten years working on optical measurements, data analysis, and evaluation. During this time, Professor Klaus Jöns's group collaborated closely with a team led by Professor Rinaldo Trotta at the Sapienza University of Rome.

"The experiment impressively demonstrates that quantum light sources based on semiconductor quantum dots could serve as a key technology for future quantum communication networks. Successful quantum teleportation between two independent quantum emitters represents a vital step towards scalable quantum relays and thus the practical implementation of a quantum internet," explained Professor Jöns, head of the 'Hybrid Photonics Quantum Devices' research group and a member of the board of the Institute for Photonic Quantum Systems (PhoQS) at Paderborn University.

Why Entanglement Matters for Quantum Communication

Entangled systems made up of multiple quantum particles offer major advantages for communication technologies. Instead of relying on a single state determined by one photon, these systems create interconnected states across multiple particles. This approach is essential for applications in secure communication, data processing, and quantum computing.

Entanglement links specific properties of photons, allowing them to share information. A state represents a unit of information being processed. "Previously, these photons came from one and the same source, i.e. the same emitter. Although there has been significant process made in recent years, using distinct quantum emitters to implement a quantum relay between independent parties had previously remained out of reach," Professor Jöns noted.

Long-Term Strategy and Advanced Technology

About ten years ago, Professors Jöns and Trotta outlined a plan for using quantum dots as sources of entangled photon pairs in communication and teleportation systems. Their latest success confirms that this long-term approach has worked.

"This result shows that our long-term strategic planning has paid off," Professor Jöns said, adding: "The combination of excellent materials science, nanofabrication and optical quantum technology was the key to our success."

Europe-Wide Collaboration Enables Precision Results

The breakthrough relied on contributions from several research centers across Europe. Quantum dots were precisely engineered at Johannes Kepler University Linz, while resonator nanofabrication was carried out by partners at the University of Würzburg. The teleportation experiments themselves took place at Sapienza University of Rome, where scientists connected two buildings using a 270m free-space optical link.

The system used GPS-assisted synchronization, ultra-fast single photon detectors, and stabilization methods to counter atmospheric turbulence. The achieved teleportation state fidelity (i.e. the quality in which quantum states are preserved during teleportation) reached up to 82 ± 1%, exceeding the classical limit by more than 10 standard deviations.

Next Step: Building a Quantum Relay

This accomplishment opens the door to the next goal, demonstrating 'entanglement swapping' between two quantum dots. Achieving this would create the first quantum relay using two deterministic sources of entangled photon pairs. Deterministic sources can reliably produce single photons almost on demand, although developing them has been a major challenge.

Parallel Advances Strengthen Quantum Research

At nearly the same time, another research team from Stuttgart and Saarbrücken reported a similar achievement using frequency conversion. Together, these results mark an important milestone for quantum research in Europe and bring the vision of a functional quantum internet closer to reality.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum teleportation between independent quantum dots over a 270-meter open-air link, marking a key step toward building scalable quantum communication networks and a quantum internet. The achievement has broad implications for ultra-secure communication and advanced quantum technologies, involving a decade-long international collaboration and detailed experimental success, fulfilling the criteria of positive real-world impact, significance, and substantive detail.
articles.personality_title: Scientists teleport photon state over 270 meters, advancing quantum networks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working for years to build a quantum internet, which uses special particles of light called photons to send information in new, super-secure ways. Quantum dots, tiny structures that emit photons, are important tools in this research.

**What happened** – For the first time, researchers successfully teleported the state of a single photon from one quantum dot to another located 270 meters away in open air. This means they transferred the photon’s properties without moving the photon itself. The experiment connected two buildings using a special optical link and involved teams from universities across Europe, including Paderborn University in Germany and Sapienza University in Rome.

**Impact** – This is a major step because it shows quantum information can be sent between separate devices, not just from the same source. The teleportation was done with high accuracy, exceeding classical limits. This success brings scientists closer to building quantum relays, devices that will help create a real quantum internet for ultra-secure communication.

**What’s next step** – The next goal is to achieve “entanglement swapping” between two quantum dots. This would allow the creation of the first true quantum relay, which can link different parts of a quantum network reliably. Achieving this will make large-scale quantum communication possible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have teleported a photon’s state over 270 meters between separate quantum dots, marking a key advance toward practical quantum communication networks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques téléportent l’état d’un photon sur 270 mètres, une avancée pour les réseaux quantiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des années, les scientifiques travaillent à la création d’un internet quantique, utilisant des particules de lumière appelées photons pour transmettre des informations de manière ultra-sécurisée. Les points quantiques, de minuscules structures émettant des photons, jouent un rôle crucial dans cette recherche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour la première fois, des chercheurs ont téléporté avec succès l’état d’un photon unique d’un point quantique à un autre situé à 270 mètres à l’air libre. Cela signifie qu’ils ont transféré les propriétés du photon sans déplacer le photon lui-même. L’expérience a relié deux bâtiments par un lien optique spécial et a réuni des équipes de plusieurs universités européennes, dont l’université de Paderborn en Allemagne et l’université Sapienza de Rome.

**Impact** – Cette avancée est importante car elle montre que l’information quantique peut être envoyée entre des dispositifs distincts, et pas seulement depuis une même source. La téléportation a été réalisée avec une grande précision, dépassant les limites classiques. Ce succès rapproche les scientifiques de la création de relais quantiques, essentiels pour un internet quantique ultra-sécurisé.

**Prochaine étape** – L’objectif suivant est de réaliser un « échange d’intrication » entre deux points quantiques. Cela permettra de créer le premier relais quantique véritable, capable de connecter différentes parties d’un réseau quantique de façon fiable. Cette étape est clé pour les communications quantiques à grande échelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont téléporté l’état d’un photon sur 270 mètres entre deux points quantiques, une avancée majeure vers des réseaux de communication quantique pratiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos teletransportan el estado de un fotón a 270 metros, avanzando en redes cuánticas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante años, los científicos han trabajado para construir un internet cuántico, que usa partículas de luz llamadas fotones para enviar información de manera muy segura. Los puntos cuánticos, estructuras diminutas que emiten fotones, son herramientas clave en esta investigación.

**Qué pasó** – Por primera vez, los investigadores teletransportaron con éxito el estado de un solo fotón de un punto cuántico a otro ubicado a 270 metros de distancia al aire libre. Esto significa que transfirieron las propiedades del fotón sin mover el fotón en sí. El experimento conectó dos edificios mediante un enlace óptico especial y contó con equipos de universidades europeas como la Universidad de Paderborn en Alemania y la Universidad Sapienza en Roma.

**Impacto** – Este es un paso importante porque demuestra que la información cuántica puede enviarse entre dispositivos separados, no solo desde una misma fuente. La teleportación se realizó con alta precisión, superando los límites clásicos. Este éxito acerca a los científicos a construir relevos cuánticos, dispositivos que ayudarán a crear un internet cuántico real para comunicación ultra segura.

**Próximo paso** – El siguiente objetivo es lograr el “intercambio de entrelazamiento” entre dos puntos cuánticos. Esto permitiría crear el primer relevo cuántico verdadero, que puede conectar diferentes partes de una red cuántica de forma confiable. Lograrlo hará posible la comunicación cuántica a gran escala.

**Frase clave** – Los investigadores teletransportaron el estado de un fotón a 270 metros entre puntos cuánticos separados, un avance clave hacia redes de comunicación cuántica prácticas.
articles.image_prompt: Two glowing, intricately patterned quantum dots connected by a shimmering, translucent beam of light spanning a gentle 270-meter aerial space between two stylized university buildings under a calm sky, rendered in warm, natural tones with fine, detailed brushwork evoking a sense of precise scientific harmony and breakthrough.

articles.title: Decarboxylative alkylation of alkenes
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 29 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10463-1A Pd-catalysed method enables regio- and diastereoselective C–H alkylation of diverse alkenes using carboxylic acids, expanding synthetic access to complex substituted alkenes.
articles.content:
Arenes and alkenes share the presence of C(sp2)–H bonds of similar bond dissociation energy, yet their reactivity differs fundamentally. Arenes undergo electrophilic substitution because of aromatic stabilization, for example, in Friedel–Crafts alkylation. By contrast, alkenes react by electrophilic addition (Fig. 1a), which prevents analogous substitution chemistry, so there is no alkene analogue to Friedel–Crafts alkylation, and the general alkylation of alkenes remains unknown. Current strategies for substituted alkene synthesis commonly rely on alkenation reactions such as the Wittig4, Horner–Wadsworth–Emmons (HWE)5,6 and Julia protocols7, which proceed from carbonyl compounds. Reductive alkylation of alkynes8,9,10 offers an alternative. Alkene cross-metathesis provides a formal alkylation pathway directly from alkenes by transalkenylidination, but the types of alkene suitable for cross-metathesis with one another must be carefully selected, and the reaction is restricted to alkene pairs of appropriate reactivity11. Moreover, E-selective cross-metathesis of 1-alkenes and 1,1-di-substituted alkenes remains unknown, and several alkene classes, such as tri-substituted and cyclic alkenes, cannot be alkylated with alkene metathesis at all due to the mechanism-based alkylidene transfer that breaks the C=C double bond before a new one is formed12,13,14. An alternative approach to alkene synthesis involves cross-coupling between alkenyl nucleophiles or electrophiles and appropriate alkyl donors. Examples include alkenyl organometallic reagents that react with alkyl (pseudo)halides or redox-active esters, or conversely, alkenyl (pseudo)halides that react in cross-coupling reactions with alkyl nucleophiles or electrophiles15,16,17. However, alkenyl nucleophiles such as Grignard reagents often require multistep preparation from alkenes, show low tolerance to functional groups, and are generally not readily available in large scope18. Alkenyl electrophiles such as halides or triflates are, while synthetically immensely versatile19, similarly difficult to access directly from alkenes, typically using multistep sequences, such as dibromination followed by elimination, which can form multiple constitutional isomers that reduce practicality20. Alkylation of alkenylthianthrenium salts is known as demonstrated in our previous work, but so far limited to a few simple, available alkylzinc reagents and restricted to terminal 1-alkenes and vinyl groups only21,22,23. Alkylation of other alkenyl sulfonium salts is confined only to styrenes24,25. Efforts to alkylate alkenes have so far been limited to electronically activated alkenes, such as styrenes26 or Michael acceptors27, and the diversity of applicable alkyl groups is narrow28. Heck-type alkylations typically involve the addition of alkyl halides or equivalents under palladium catalysis26 or through photo-induced radical pathways but are generally limited to styrenes or other activated alkenes29,30,31. Alkyl carboxylic acids are excellent alkyl sources because they offer a massive, structurally diverse pool of bench-stable resources. Alkenes and alkyl carboxylic acids are among the most abundant functional groups in organic synthesis, and a general method to use them in combination for C(sp2)−C(sp3) bond formation is hitherto unknown. Here we report a regio- and diastereoselective reaction to access alkylated alkenes that works across the various alkene substitution patterns and electronic environments with a large range of alkyl groups (Fig. 1b). The fundamental novelty reported herein is the recognition of a new retron for alkene alkylation, and the development of a polar decarboxylative alkylation distinct in mechanism from the established radical-mediated carbon–carbon bond-forming processes from activated carboxylic acids32. A combination of chemoselective alkylzinc formation from carboxylic acids through redox-active esters and diastereoselective alkene thianthrenation unlocks valuable chemical space and provides access to molecular architectures that are challenging to synthesize using existing methods (Fig. 1c).

Fig. 1: Development of polar decarboxylative strategy for selective alkene alkylation. The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI. Full size image a, Alkene reactivity for addition reaction and challenges with C−H alkylation of alkenes. b, Scope of alkene alkylation. c, Pd-catalysed formal decarboxylative alkylation of alkenes. d, Challenges with radical-mediated cross-coupling between two transient radicals formed from alkenyl thianthrenium salts and redox-active esters, and development of the polar decarboxylative C(sp2)–C(sp3) cross-coupling.

Alkene C−H bonds can be substituted through thianthrenation, which proceeds by an inverse-electron-demand hetero Diels–Alder cycloaddition between the thianthrenium dication and the alkene. The resulting bicyclic dicationic intermediate undergoes base-mediated elimination through an irreversible E1cB pathway to deliver the alkenyl thianthrenium salt20,33. This addition–elimination sequence effectively achieves vinylic C−H substitution in a single step to provide direct access to alkenyl electrophiles from simple alkenes. Carboxylic acids, on conversion to redox-active esters, can serve as versatile alkyl group donors34. However, all existing methods for decarboxylative alkylation proceed through single-electron pathways, which generate alkyl radicals34,35,36,37,38,39. Although alkenyl thianthrenium salts can also undergo single-electron reduction (E p = −1.68 V compared with Ag/AgNO 3 in DMF), we recognized the potential difficulty to couple two non-persistent radicals40 (Fig. 1d); selective radical–radical cross-coupling is challenging, and, to date, such an alkyl–alkenyl bond-forming transformation has not been disclosed.

In line with the analysis of challenging cross-coupling by conventional one-electron pathways, we observed predominant protodethianthrenation of the alkenyl electrophile and hydrodecarboxylation of the redox-active ester (Supplementary Information), when we evaluated reaction conditions akin to single-electron reduction of redox-active esters with common Ni- or Fe-based catalysts33,35,37,38 (Supplementary Table 1). These results underscore the challenge in chemoselectivity for a pathway that proceeds through C(sp2)−C(sp3) bond formation from carbon-based radicals, possibly due to competitive reduction of both electrophiles and the generation of two transient radical species. A polar, two-electron alternative through a Pd(0)/Pd(II) redox pathway with alkenylthianthrenium salts as electrophiles could improve this chemoselectivity, yet requires the conversion of redox-active esters to a carbanion synthon. A previous work41 identified initial evidence for the conversion of alkyl redox-active esters to alkylzinc species, which has remained unexplored by the synthetic community since 2019. Based on an analysis in ref. 42, in which the reduction potential of Zn as a function of solvent was studied, we developed the alkylzinc formation from redox-active ester to ultimately result in successful formation of persistent alkylzinc species, suitable for efficient palladium-catalysed cross-coupling with alkenylthianthrenium salts (Fig. 1d). To our knowledge, this transformation represents the first example of a polar decarboxylative C(sp2)−C(sp3) cross-coupling, enabled by formation of a stable alkylzinc intermediate that replaces the commonly used fleeting carbon-based radical as the alkylating agent. Although formation of the alkylzinc species probably proceeds through single-electron transfer (SET) from zinc on account of the electronic configuration of zinc metal, this pathway is distinct from the radical pathway in other decarboxylative bond formation, in which the carbon radical is engaged in oxidative ligation to a transition metal complex, and does not share the same chemoselectivity constraints because persistent alkylzinc species are generated instead.

Reduction of redox-active esters with zinc in solvents such as acetonitrile (MeCN) and ethyl acetate have been used for the generation of alkyl radicals43,44. Yet, reaction of redox-active ester RAE-1 and Zn in DMF generated 52% of the corresponding alkylzinc species I-1 within the first 2 h (Fig. 2a); analogous reduction of redox-active esters with Zn in solvents that are commonly used in cross-coupling chemistry, such as THF and MeCN, did not lead to alkylzinc nucleophiles synthetically useful in this transformation45,46 (Supplementary Table 10). In-cage recombination of the incipient alkyl radical with Zn+ after the first single-electron transfer and stabilization of the zinc organometallic in the polar aprotic medium may be responsible for the distinct reduction chemistry. Addition of the catalyst and alkenyl thianthrenium salt after 2 h to the reaction mixture, resulted in optimal yields for the cross-coupling (Fig. 2b). Combination of all reagents at once resulted in predominant protodethianthrenation (Supplementary Table 6). This result indicates that alkylzinc formation is slower than oxidative addition of the alkenyl thianthrenium salt to Pd(0), and initial formation of alkylzinc is essential for efficient cross-coupling. The reaction can also proceed if the redox-active ester is prepared in situ from the corresponding carboxylic acid, albeit in lower yield; for example, compound 1 was prepared in 40% yield without and in 75% yield with previous isolation of the redox-active ester (Supplementary Information). Attempts to replace Zn with alternative reductants such as Mn, Mg and organic donors such as TDAE failed to produce a cross-coupled product (Supplementary Table 5).

Fig. 2: Reaction development for selective alkene alkylation. The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI. Full size image a, Alkylzinc formation from redox-active esters. b, Reaction conditions for two-step alkylation of alkene. Cross-coupling reactions were conducted on 0.2 mmol scale.

A large variety of different alkenes can be alkylated (Fig. 3). Because the palladium-catalysed cross-coupling from alkenyl thianthrenium salts proceeds stereospecifically with regard to alkene geometry, and thianthrenation generally proceeds with high E-selectivity (>20:1 for 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 12), products are produced with high stereochemical selectivity. Even 1,1-di-substituted alkenes deliver E-selective alkylation, for example, 10:1 for 23 and >20:1 for 20. At present, neither compound class can be accessed through alkene cross-metathesis because a general approach for E-selective alkene cross-metathesis is not yet known. The Wickens group reported a method for obtaining Z-selective thianthrenation22; in our protocol, Z-alkenyl thianthrenium salts can be converted to Z alkenes under identical reaction conditions as for the cross-coupling for E alkenes (Supplementary Information). Electron-rich alkenes can be selectively alkylated in the presence of electron-poor alkenes, such as α,β-unsaturated ketones (20, 23 and 24), because thianthrenation occurs preferentially at the most electron-rich double bond. The method is applicable to both simple feedstock alkenes and structurally complex substrates. Late-stage alkylation of terpenes such as rose oxide (18) and citronellol (21) could therefore be achieved. Aldehyde and ketone functionalities, such as those in carvone (20) and perillaldehyde (23), respectively, are well tolerated under the reaction conditions without the need for protecting groups. Terminal alkynes do not pose a chemoselectivity issue, and selective alkylation occurs exclusively at the alkene moiety without competing functionalization of the alkyne (4). Alkylation of tri-substituted alkenes remains synthetically challenging, with almost no precedent in the literature. The protocol here allows the selective installation of primary and secondary alkyl groups onto internal di- and tri-substituted alkenes, providing a general and predictable approach to highly substituted alkene motifs47 (5, 10, 14, 21 and 22). The reaction accommodates diverse alkyl partners, including strained rings, saturated heterocycles and complex derivatives such as indomethacin (11) and baclofen (25). Oxyacids, such as 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (17) and menthyloxyacetic acid (19), can also serve as alkyl sources. The scope is limited to primary and secondary alkyl groups because tertiary redox-active esters, although reduced by zinc, do not form organozinc intermediates effectively under these conditions.

Fig. 3: Scope for Pd-catalysed two-step alkylation of alkenes. The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI. Full size image Reactions for the thianthrenation of alkenes were conducted on a 0.5 mmol scale (see Supplementary Information for detailed reaction conditions). Cross-coupling reactions were conducted on a 0.2 mmol scale. General conditions for cross-coupling: alkenyl thianthrenium salt (0.20 mmol), redox-active ester (0.44 mmol), Zn dust (0.60 mmol, mesh size <60 µm), PdCl 2 (amphos) 2 (4.0 mol%) and DMF (0.125 M). TTO, thianthrene-S-oxide; TfOH, triflic acid; TFAA, trifluoroacetic anhydride.

Redox-active esters (E p = −1.59 V compared with Ag/AgNO 3 in DMF) and alkenyl thianthrenium salts (E p = −1.68 V compared with Ag/AgNO 3 in DMF) exhibit comparable reduction potentials (Supplementary Figs. 9 and 11). However, kinetic analysis showed that zinc reduces redox-active esters significantly faster than alkenyl thianthrenium salts (Fig. 4a,b). We observed an autocatalytic effect of soluble Zn(II) for the reaction of the redox-active ester with metallic zinc and established that addition of alkylzinc accelerates chemoselective reduction, possibly through coordination as Lewis acid to the redox-active ester48 (Supplementary Figs. 14–16). Analysis of the zinc-mediated reduction of RAE-1 by 1H and 13C-NMR spectroscopy in DMF-d 7 suggests that a persistent alkylzinc species is formed (Supplementary Figs. 3 and 4). Electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry analysis detects species consistent with alkylzinc coordinated by two DMF molecules (Supplementary Fig. 7), which suggests that solvent coordination stabilizes the organozinc intermediate. Analysis of 1H NMR spectra of the zinc-mediated reduction of redox-active esters revealed three major side products: alkane formation by protodecarboxylation of the redox-active ester, the corresponding carboxylic acid and a homocoupling product, all of which suggesting that decarboxylation proceeds radically, during formation of alkylzinc as the key alkylating agent (Supplementary Information). By contrast, no spectroscopic evidence for alkylzinc formation was observed when the reduction was conducted in THF-d 8 at either 25 °C or 60 °C (Supplementary Fig. 6). To quantify the in situ-generated alkylzinc intermediate, iodine (I 2 ) was added to the reaction mixture of redox-active ester and zinc in DMF after 2 h, which resulted in 52% yield of the alkyl iodide derived from RAE-1 (Supplementary Information). Addition of 10 mol% I 2 to the alkylation reaction increased yield by 8% (Fig. 4c), consistent with in situ zinc activation. Replacement of the redox-active ester with an alkylzinc reagent directly prepared from alkyl iodide afforded cross-coupled product with 78% yield at 60 °C under otherwise identical conditions (Fig. 4d), which also supports in situ-formed alkylzinc reagent as the alkylating species for the cross-coupling reaction.

Fig. 4: Mechanistic studies. The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI. Full size image a, Validation and structure of the in situ-formed alkylzinc species from redox-active esters (asterisk indicates mass spectrometry experiments were conducted in non-deuterated DMF). b, Control experiment for zinc-mediated reduction of alkenyl thianthrenium salt at room temperature in DMF. c, Iodine quenching experiment for the quantification of alkylzinc species. d, Cross-coupling under standard conditions with alkyl iodide and alkenyl thianthrenium salt. e, Plausible catalytic cycle for the polar decarboxylative cross-coupling between alkenyl thianthrenium salt and redox-active ester. S, solvent (DMF).

Cross-electrophile coupling is an emerging area of research that offers access to a broader pool of structurally diverse precursors than traditional electrophile–nucleophile cross-couplings16. In particular, decarboxylative cross-coupling between redox-active esters with other electrophiles is attractive because carboxylic acids provide abundant, bench-stable alkyl sources. Over the past decade, numerous decarboxylative strategies for C(sp2)−C(sp3) and C(sp3)−C(sp3) bond formation have transformed synthetic planning and expanded the accessible chemical space32. To date, decarboxylative cross-coupling approaches to C−C bond formation have been guided predominantly by radical-based retrosynthetic logic. Here, we introduce a polar decarboxylative cross-coupling pathway that generates a persistent anionic intermediate from carboxylic acids, which enables the selective coupling of two otherwise incompatible transient radicals. The development of this polar decarboxylative manifold introduces a complementary, polarity-driven disconnection strategy for C−C bond formation and establishes a new retrosynthetic logic for the alkylation of alkenes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in synthetic organic chemistry: a novel Pd-catalysed polar decarboxylative alkylation method that enables regio- and diastereoselective C–H alkylation of diverse alkenes using abundant carboxylic acids. This method overcomes longstanding challenges in alkene alkylation, expands synthetic access to complex substituted alkenes, and offers broad applicability including late-stage functionalization of complex molecules. The discovery has broad implications for chemical synthesis, pharmaceuticals, and materials science, representing a meaningful advance with tangible benefits for society and industry.
articles.personality_title: New palladium method simplifies making complex alkene molecules
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alkenes are common chemical building blocks used to make many products, but changing their structure by adding alkyl groups has been difficult. Traditional methods either don’t work well or require many steps, limiting the types of alkenes that can be modified.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a new way to directly attach alkyl groups to alkenes using a palladium (Pd) catalyst and carboxylic acids, which are cheap and easy-to-find chemicals. This method uses a special two-step process: first, converting alkenes into a reactive form called alkenyl thianthrenium salts, and second, creating alkylzinc intermediates from carboxylic acids. These then combine to form new carbon–carbon bonds with high precision, controlling both where and how the alkyl groups attach.

**Impact** – This breakthrough lets chemists add diverse alkyl groups to a wide range of alkenes, including types that were previously hard or impossible to modify. The process works with complex molecules, tolerates many chemical groups, and produces products with controlled shapes, which is important for making medicines and materials. It opens new paths for designing molecules more efficiently.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely explore using this method to create new drugs and materials, as well as improve the process to work with even more types of alkyl groups. Further studies may also optimize the reaction conditions and scale it up for industrial use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new palladium-catalyzed reaction enables precise and efficient addition of alkyl groups to various alkenes, expanding the toolkit for making complex molecules.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode au palladium simplifie la création de molécules d’alcènes complexes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les alcènes sont des blocs de construction chimiques courants utilisés pour fabriquer de nombreux produits, mais modifier leur structure en ajoutant des groupes alkyles a toujours été difficile. Les méthodes traditionnelles sont souvent inefficaces ou nécessitent plusieurs étapes, limitant les types d’alcènes modifiables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle méthode pour attacher directement des groupes alkyles aux alcènes grâce à un catalyseur au palladium (Pd) et des acides carboxyliques, des substances bon marché et faciles à trouver. Cette méthode utilise un procédé en deux étapes : d’abord, transformer les alcènes en sels d’alkényl thianthrenium réactifs, puis créer des intermédiaires alkylzinc à partir d’acides carboxyliques. Ces deux éléments se combinent ensuite pour former de nouvelles liaisons carbone–carbone avec une grande précision, contrôlant la position et la forme des groupes alkyles.

**Impact** – Cette avancée permet d’ajouter une grande variété de groupes alkyles à un large éventail d’alcènes, y compris ceux qui étaient auparavant difficiles ou impossibles à modifier. Le procédé fonctionne avec des molécules complexes, tolère de nombreux groupes chimiques et produit des produits avec une forme contrôlée, ce qui est important pour la fabrication de médicaments et de matériaux. Il ouvre de nouvelles voies pour concevoir des molécules plus efficacement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer l’utilisation de cette méthode pour créer de nouveaux médicaments et matériaux, ainsi qu’améliorer le procédé pour qu’il fonctionne avec encore plus de types de groupes alkyles. Des études supplémentaires pourraient aussi optimiser les conditions de réaction et permettre une production à plus grande échelle.

**Une phrase clé** – Une nouvelle réaction catalysée au palladium permet d’ajouter avec précision et efficacité des groupes alkyles à divers alcènes, élargissant les outils pour fabriquer des molécules complexes.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método con paladio facilita la creación de moléculas complejas de alquenos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los alquenos son bloques químicos comunes usados para fabricar muchos productos, pero modificar su estructura añadiendo grupos alquilo ha sido difícil. Los métodos tradicionales no funcionan bien o requieren muchos pasos, limitando los tipos de alquenos que se pueden modificar.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron una nueva forma de unir directamente grupos alquilo a alquenos usando un catalizador de paladio (Pd) y ácidos carboxílicos, que son químicos baratos y fáciles de conseguir. Este método usa un proceso en dos pasos: primero, convertir los alquenos en una forma reactiva llamada sales de alquenilo tiantrenio, y segundo, crear intermediarios alquilzinc a partir de los ácidos carboxílicos. Estos se combinan para formar nuevos enlaces carbono–carbono con mucha precisión, controlando dónde y cómo se unen los grupos alquilo.

**Impacto** – Este avance permite a los químicos añadir diversos grupos alquilo a una amplia variedad de alquenos, incluyendo tipos que antes eran difíciles o imposibles de modificar. El proceso funciona con moléculas complejas, tolera muchos grupos químicos y produce productos con formas controladas, lo cual es importante para fabricar medicinas y materiales. Abre nuevas formas de diseñar moléculas de manera más eficiente.

**Qué sigue** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán usar este método para crear nuevos medicamentos y materiales, además de mejorar el proceso para que funcione con más tipos de grupos alquilo. También podrían optimizar las condiciones de reacción y escalar la producción para uso industrial.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva reacción catalizada por paladio permite añadir grupos alquilo con precisión y eficiencia a varios alquenos, ampliando las herramientas para crear moléculas complejas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two distinct, intricately intertwined molecular structures symbolizing alkenes and alkyl carboxylic acids, connected by a glowing, delicate bridge representing the novel polar decarboxylative alkylation reaction, set against a softly textured abstract background of muted natural colors that evoke harmony and precise chemical transformation.

articles.title: Recycling of spin-triplet excitons in organic photovoltaics
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 29 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10419-5Triplet excitons in OPVs can redissociate via the charge-transfer state into free carriers, reducing losses and improving efficiency, enabled by molecular design controlling singlet–triplet energetics through exciton delocalization.
articles.content: A.K.Y.J. is grateful for sponsorship by the Lee Shau-Kee Chair Professorship in Materials Science, and support from APRC grants (grant nos. 9380086, 9610419, 9610440, 9610492, 9610508) of the City University of Hong Kong; MHKJFS, TCFS and MRP (grant nos. MHP/054/23, GHP/121/22SZ, MRP/040/21X, respectively) grants from the Innovation and Technology Commission of Hong Kong; and GRF (grant nos. 11307621, 11316422, 11308625) and CRS (grant nos. CRS_CityU104/23 and CRS_HKUST203/23) grants from the Research Grants Council of Hong Kong. This work was partially financially supported by City University of Hong Kong (grant no. 9610739) via the ‘Fostering Innovation for Resilience and Sustainable Transformation’ project, which is officially endorsed by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization, under the International Decade of Sciences for Sustainable Development (2024–2033). D.L. is grateful for support from the Collaborative Research Equipment Grant (grant no. C1015-21EF) via the Research Grants Council of Hong Kong. C.S.L. is grateful for support from the General Research Fund from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (grant no. CityU 11303923). S.W.T. is grateful for the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 62474151). X.K.C. gratefully acknowledges financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant no. 2022YFB4200600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant nos. W2511063 and 52473190), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant no. BK20240042), the Science and Technology Project of Suzhou (grant no. ZXL2024394), the Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano and Soft Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, and the 111 Project. We also gratefully acknowledge Y. Li, Y. Wang, Z. Yue and T. Xia from the City University of Hong Kong for their assistance with ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy, photoluminescence and electroluminescence measurements, and cell characterizations; M. Yan from the Nanjing University for their assistance with spectroscopic measurements; R. Walia from the Soochow University for their assistance with theoretical calculations; and S.-H. Jang from the University of Washington for their assistance with writing the manuscript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement in organic photovoltaics (OPVs) that improves efficiency by recycling spin-triplet excitons, which can lead to more efficient solar energy conversion. This breakthrough has broad implications for renewable energy technology and sustainable development, supported by substantial research and international collaboration.
articles.personality_title: New method boosts organic solar cell efficiency by recycling energy particles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Organic photovoltaics (OPVs) are solar cells made from carbon-based materials. They are cheaper and more flexible than traditional solar panels but usually less efficient at turning sunlight into electricity.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered a way to recycle special energy particles inside OPVs called spin-triplet excitons. These particles can usually get lost, reducing the solar cell’s efficiency. The team designed molecules that help these excitons break apart again into free charges that generate electricity, improving how much sunlight the cells can convert.

**Impact** – This discovery helps reduce energy loss in organic solar cells, making them more efficient. It could lead to better, more affordable solar panels that work well in various conditions. This progress supports cleaner energy by making solar power technology more practical and sustainable.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve molecular designs to boost recycling of excitons even more. They will also test these improved solar cells in real-world conditions and explore ways to produce them at a larger scale for everyday use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found a new way to recycle energy inside organic solar cells, making them more efficient and closer to widespread use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle méthode améliore l'efficacité des cellules solaires organiques en recyclant les particules d'énergie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les photovoltaïques organiques (OPV) sont des cellules solaires fabriquées à partir de matériaux à base de carbone. Elles sont moins chères et plus flexibles que les panneaux solaires traditionnels, mais souvent moins efficaces pour convertir la lumière en électricité.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert comment recycler des particules d'énergie spéciales à l'intérieur des OPV, appelées excitons spin-triplet. Ces particules sont généralement perdues, ce qui réduit l'efficacité des cellules solaires. L'équipe a conçu des molécules qui aident ces excitons à se séparer à nouveau en charges libres générant de l'électricité, améliorant ainsi la conversion de la lumière.

**Impact** – Cette découverte réduit la perte d'énergie dans les cellules solaires organiques, les rendant plus efficaces. Elle pourrait conduire à des panneaux solaires meilleurs et moins chers, fonctionnant bien dans diverses conditions. Ce progrès soutient une énergie plus propre en rendant la technologie solaire plus pratique et durable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer la conception des molécules pour optimiser le recyclage des excitons. Ils testeront aussi ces cellules solaires améliorées en conditions réelles et chercheront à les produire à plus grande échelle pour un usage quotidien.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont trouvé un nouveau moyen de recycler l'énergie dans les cellules solaires organiques, les rendant plus efficaces et plus proches d'une utilisation généralisée.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método mejora la eficiencia de las células solares orgánicas reciclando partículas de energía
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares orgánicas (OPV) están hechas de materiales basados en carbono. Son más baratas y flexibles que los paneles solares tradicionales, pero suelen ser menos eficientes para convertir la luz en electricidad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron cómo reciclar unas partículas de energía especiales dentro de las OPV llamadas excitones de espín triplete. Estas partículas suelen perderse, lo que reduce la eficiencia. El equipo diseñó moléculas que ayudan a que estos excitones se separen en cargas libres que generan electricidad, mejorando la conversión de la luz.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento reduce la pérdida de energía en las células solares orgánicas, haciéndolas más eficientes. Podría llevar a paneles solares mejores y más asequibles que funcionen bien en distintas condiciones. Este avance apoya la energía limpia al hacer la tecnología solar más práctica y sostenible.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando el diseño molecular para aumentar el reciclaje de excitones. También probarán estas células solares mejoradas en condiciones reales y buscarán producirlas a mayor escala para uso cotidiano.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos encontraron una nueva forma de reciclar energía dentro de las células solares orgánicas, haciéndolas más eficientes y más cerca de su uso generalizado.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interconnected glowing organic molecules forming a delicate lattice that subtly resembles solar cells, with vibrant, flowing energy waves symbolizing the recycling of spin-triplet excitons, rendered in soft natural hues of amber, green, and blue against a gentle abstract background evoking sustainable innovation and scientific collaboration.

articles.title: Digital quantum magnetism on a trapped-ion quantum computer
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 29 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10445-3Simulations performed using Quantinuum’s H2 trapped-ion quantum computer for observing thermalization on challenging timescales demonstrate the usefulness of digital quantum computers for investigating continuous-time dynamics in regimes that classical simulation methods find difficult.
articles.content: We acknowledge the entire Quantinuum team for their many contributions towards successful operation of the H2 quantum computer and Honeywell for fabricating the trap used in this experiment. We thank K. Meichanetzidis, C. Langer, G. Vittorini, S. Erickson, J. Hostetter and D. Liefer for helpful comments on the manuscript and D. Mills for useful discussions. S.A.M. contributed to the construction of the experimental apparatus while at Quantinuum. Fermioniq was supported by the Dutch National Growth Fund (NGF), as part of the Quantum Delta NL programme. W.K., M.K. and F.P. acknowledge support from the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) under Germany’s Excellence Strategy-EXC-2111-390814868, TRR 360 - 492547816 and DFG grant nos. KN1254/1-2, KN1254/2-1 and FOR 5522 (project-id 499180199), the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (grant agreement no. 851161), the European Union (grant agreement no. 101169765), as well as the Munich Quantum Valley, which is supported by the Bavarian state government with funds from the Hightech Agenda Bayern Plus. E.G. acknowledges funding by the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy (StMWi) under project Bench-QC (DIK0425/01). M.S.R. acknowledges funding from the 2024 Google PhD Fellowship, the NCCR MARVEL, a National Centre of Competence in Research, financed by the Swiss National Science Foundation (grant number 205602), and the Swiss National Science Foundation (grant number 200021-219329). Sparse Pauli dynamics simulation work at Caltech was supported by the US Department of Energy, Office of Science, National Quantum Information Science Research Centers, Quantum Systems Accelerator. The belief propagation tensor network algorithm development at Caltech was supported by the US Department of Energy, Office of Science, Basic Energy Sciences and Advanced Scientific Computing Research, Quantum Utility through Advanced Computational Quantum Algorithms.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement using a trapped-ion quantum computer to simulate thermalization on challenging timescales, demonstrating the practical usefulness of digital quantum computers for studying complex continuous-time dynamics that classical methods struggle with. This breakthrough has broad implications for advancing quantum computing technology and scientific research.
articles.personality_title: New quantum computer simulates tricky magnetic behavior
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to understand how tiny magnets behave over time, which is hard to study using normal computers because the calculations get too complicated.

**What happened** – Researchers used a special type of quantum computer called a trapped-ion quantum computer, made by a company named Quantinuum, to simulate how magnetic systems change over time. They focused on a process called thermalization, which describes how systems settle into balance. This experiment was done recently and showed the computer could handle these complex simulations.

**Impact** – This is important because normal computers struggle to simulate these continuous changes accurately. Using a digital quantum computer makes it possible to study these systems in ways not done before, opening new paths for understanding physics and improving quantum technology.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this approach to explore other difficult problems in physics and chemistry. The success also pushes development of better quantum computers that can solve real-world challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A trapped-ion quantum computer successfully simulated complex magnetic processes, showing digital quantum computers can tackle problems beyond the reach of classical methods.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel ordinateur quantique simule un comportement magnétique complexe
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment se comportent de petits aimants au fil du temps, ce qui est difficile à étudier avec des ordinateurs classiques car les calculs deviennent trop compliqués.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé un ordinateur quantique spécial appelé ordinateur quantique à ions piégés, fabriqué par la société Quantinuum, pour simuler comment les systèmes magnétiques évoluent dans le temps. Ils se sont concentrés sur un processus appelé thermalisation, qui décrit comment un système atteint un équilibre. Cette expérience récente a montré que cet ordinateur pouvait gérer ces simulations complexes.

**Impact** – C’est important car les ordinateurs classiques ont du mal à simuler ces changements continus avec précision. Utiliser un ordinateur quantique numérique permet d’étudier ces systèmes d’une manière nouvelle, ouvrant la voie à une meilleure compréhension de la physique et au progrès des technologies quantiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette méthode pour explorer d’autres problèmes difficiles en physique et en chimie. Ce succès encourage aussi le développement d’ordinateurs quantiques plus performants pour résoudre des défis concrets.

**Une phrase clé** – Un ordinateur quantique à ions piégés a simulé avec succès des processus magnétiques complexes, montrant que les ordinateurs quantiques numériques peuvent résoudre des problèmes hors de portée des méthodes classiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva computadora cuántica simula comportamiento magnético complejo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo se comportan pequeños imanes con el tiempo, lo cual es difícil de estudiar con computadoras normales porque los cálculos se vuelven muy complicados.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores usaron una computadora cuántica especial llamada computadora cuántica de iones atrapados, creada por la empresa Quantinuum, para simular cómo cambian los sistemas magnéticos con el tiempo. Se enfocaron en un proceso llamado termalización, que describe cómo los sistemas llegan a un equilibrio. Este experimento reciente mostró que la computadora puede manejar estas simulaciones complejas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque las computadoras normales tienen dificultades para simular estos cambios continuos con precisión. Usar una computadora cuántica digital hace posible estudiar estos sistemas de formas nuevas, abriendo caminos para entender mejor la física y mejorar la tecnología cuántica.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este método para explorar otros problemas difíciles en física y química. Este éxito también impulsa el desarrollo de mejores computadoras cuánticas para resolver desafíos reales.

**Una frase clave** – Una computadora cuántica de iones atrapados simuló con éxito procesos magnéticos complejos, demostrando que las computadoras cuánticas digitales pueden resolver problemas fuera del alcance de los métodos clásicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate network of glowing ion traps and quantum circuits, symbolized by softly illuminated, interconnected crystalline structures floating above a serene, abstract landscape, with subtle pulses of light representing quantum magnetism flowing through the connections.
